quality.rationale: 基于任意度规的动量空间相体积元推导，需综合运用张量变换律、度规行列式性质及质量壳δ函数积分技巧，物理动机明确且逻辑链条完整。

origin.question: Problem 3.33. What is the invariant volume element of contravariant momentum $\mathsf{d}^4\mathbf{P}$ for 4-dimensional momentum space? What is the invariant 3-volume "on the mass shell", i.e. when the constraint $(-\mathbf{P}\cdot \mathbf{P})^{\frac{1}{2}} = \mathfrak{m}$ is imposed?
origin.answer: $\\mathrm{d}^4 P = (-g)^{\\frac{1}{2}} \\mathrm{d} P^x \\mathrm{d} P^y \\mathrm{d} P^z \\mathrm{d} P^t$; $\\mathrm{d}^3 P = (-g)^{\\frac{1}{2}} \\mathrm{d} P^x \\mathrm{d} P^y \\mathrm{d} P^z \\left(\\frac{m}{-P_t}\\right)$
origin.solution:
Solution 3.33. Since the contravariant momentum vector $\mathbf{P}^{\alpha}$ transforms just like the contravariant displacement vector $\mathbf{x}^{\alpha}$ , the 4-dimensional momentum element must be of the same form as the invariant 4-volume element (Problem 3.32), so
$$
\mathrm {d}^{4} P = (- g)^{\frac{1}{2}} \mathrm {d} P^{x} \mathrm {d} P^{y} \mathrm {d} P^{z} \mathrm {d} P^{t}.
$$
For the 3-volume element we multiply by a δ-function of the constraint and integrate over $\mathbf{P}^t$ :
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm {d}^{3} P = \int \delta \left[ \left(- g_{\alpha \beta} P^{\alpha} P^{\beta}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}} - m \right] (- g)^{\frac{1}{2}} \mathrm {d} P^{x} \mathrm {d} P^{y} \mathrm {d} P^{z} \mathrm {d} P^{t} \\
= (- g)^{\frac{1}{2}} \mathrm {d} P^{x} \mathrm {d} P^{y} \mathrm {d} P^{z} \times \left[ - \frac {1} {2} \left(- g_{\alpha \beta} P^{\alpha} P^{\beta}\right)^{- \frac {1}{2}} \cdot 2 g_{t \alpha} P^{\alpha} \right]^{- 1}. \\
\end{array}
$$
(Here we have used the identity
$$
\int \delta (f (x)) d x = \frac {1} {| f^{\prime} (x_{1}) |}
$$
where $\mathbf{x}_1$ is a zero of f.) Rewriting the term in brackets, we get
$$
\mathrm {d}^{3} P = (- g)^{\frac{1}{2}} \mathrm {d} P^{x} \mathrm {d} P^{y} \mathrm {d} P^{z} \left(\frac {m}{- P_{t}}\right).
$$
Notice that in a local orthonormal frame comoving with the particles in momentum space, this reduces to
$$
\mathrm {d}^{3} P = \mathrm {d} P^{x} \mathrm {d} P^{y} \mathrm {d} P^{z}
$$
as it should.
Sometimes $\mathbf{d}^3\mathbf{P}$ is renormalized by dividing by $m$ . This gives an invariant volume element which is valid for massless particles as well.
